column6: {"recipes":[{"name":"Gochujang Tofu Rice Bowl","emoji":"🍚","desc":"Spicy glazed tofu over fluffy rice","add":[{"name_en":"Green onions"},{"name_en":"Garlic"},{"name_en":"Soy sauce"}]},{"name":"Crispy Baked Tofu Skewers","emoji":"🍢","desc":"Savory oven-baked tofu with kick","add":[{"name_en":"Bell pepper"},{"name_en":"Pineapple"},{"name_en":"Sesame seeds"}]},{"name":"Gochujang Tofu Soup","emoji":"🍲","desc":"Warm, spicy broth with tofu","add":[{"name_en":"Mushrooms"},{"name_en":"Spinach"},{"name_en":"Vegetable broth"}]}]}
column10: All 3 recipes are practical home-cooking and use tofu as a central ingredient. None of the added ingredients are gochujang, sesame oil, or rice. The recipes are Korean-influenced via gochujang. However, only 1 of 3 is clearly a recognizably Korean dish ('Gochujang Tofu Soup' is vague and 'Crispy Baked Tofu Skewers' is not recognizably Korean), so the 'at least 2 of 3 recognizably Korean dishes' criterion is not met. Descriptions are concise and under 8 words.

column1: chicken thigh + Mexican items + vegetarian profile — conflict handling
column6: {"recipes":[{"name":"Mushroom Taco Wraps","emoji":"🌮","desc":"Savory vegetarian tacos with bright herbs","add":[{"name_en":"Mushrooms"},{"name_en":"Black beans"},{"name_en":"Avocado"}]},{"name":"Crispy Cauliflower Quesadillas","emoji":"🫓","desc":"Melty, crispy, tangy handheld quesadillas","add":[{"name_en":"Cauliflower"},{"name_en":"Cheese"},{"name_en":"Salsa"}]},{"name":"Lime Cilantro Rice Bowl","emoji":"🍚","desc":"Fresh bowl with zesty roasted vegetables","add":[{"name_en":"Rice"},{"name_en":"Bell pepper"},{"name_en":"Corn"}]}]}
column10: The output provides vegetarian alternatives (approach b) and the recipes lean Mexican, added ingredients don't duplicate existing items, and descriptions are concise. However, the rubric requires that if taking approach (b), the model should 'note the conflict and offer vegetarian alternatives.' The output silently provides vegetarian recipes without acknowledging the conflict between the user's chicken thigh request and their vegetarian restriction. This partial handling is not fully acceptable per the rubric's requirement that 'ignoring the conflict entirely is NOT acceptable.'

column1: bok choy + garlic/oyster sauce — authentic Chinese, not fusion
column6: {"recipes":[{"name":"Garlic Oyster Bok Choy Stir-Fry","emoji":"🥢","desc":"Quick savory side with glossy sauce","add":[{"name_en":"Sesame oil"},{"name_en":"Soy sauce"},{"name_en":"Cornstarch"}]},{"name":"Bok Choy Noodle Soup","emoji":"🍜","desc":"Light comforting bowl with greens","add":[{"name_en":"Rice noodles"},{"name_en":"Ginger"},{"name_en":"Chicken broth"}]},{"name":"Roasted Bok Choy with Garlic","emoji":"🔥","desc":"Charred greens with rich drizzle","add":[{"name_en":"Butter"},{"name_en":"Lemon"},{"name_en":"Parmesan"}]}]}
column10: Recipe 3 (Roasted Bok Choy with butter, lemon, Parmesan) is clearly a Western/fusion dish, violating the requirement for authentic Chinese recipes and the explicit instruction against westernized dishes. While Recipes 1 and 2 are acceptable, having a full third of the output be exactly the kind of fusion cooking the rubric warns against is a significant failure.
column16: Fails multiple rubric points: recipe 2 ('Roasted Bok Choy with Salmon') is westernized/sheet-pan style rather than authentically Chinese, so at least 2 of 3 are not clearly recognizably Chinese enough. Descriptions exceed 8 words for all three entries. Recipe 2 also does not clearly present bok choy as a key ingredient. It does satisfy the 'no garlic or oyster sauce in added ingredients' requirement, and recipes are practical home cooking.
column18: {"recipes": [{"name": "Garlic Bok Choy Stir-Fry", "emoji": "🥢", "desc": "Quick savory stir-fry with silky tofu", "add": [{"name_en": "Tofu"}, {"name_en": "Sesame Oil"}, {"name_en": "Soy Sauce"}]}, {"name": "Bok Choy Miso Soup", "emoji": "🍜", "desc": "Warm comforting Japanese-style broth bowl", "add": [{"name_en": "Miso Paste"}, {"name_en": "Dashi Stock"}, {"name_en": "Green Onions"}]}, {"name": "Roasted Bok Choy with Noodles", "emoji": "🍝", "desc": "Caramelized roasted greens over chewy noodles", "add": [{"name_en": "Udon Noodles"}, {"name_en": "Chili Flakes"}, {"name_en": "Ginger"}]}]}
column22: Only 1 of 3 recipes (Garlic Bok Choy Stir-Fry) is recognizably Chinese. Miso soup is Japanese, and roasted bok choy with udon noodles is Japanese-leaning. The rubric requires at least 2 of 3 to be recognizably Chinese, and that recipes be authentically Chinese given the user's garlic and oyster sauce. This key criterion is not met.
